[[ar:أماتيراسو]][[ary:أماطيراسو]][[az:Amaterasu]][[bcl:Amaterasu]][[be_x_old:Аматэрасу]][[be:Аматэрасу]][[bew:Amatérasu]][[bg:Аматерасу]][[ca:Amaterasu]][[cs:Amaterasu]][[da:Amaterasu]][[de:Amaterasu]][[el:Αματεράσου]][[en:Amaterasu]][[eo:Amaterasu]][[es:Amaterasu]][[et:Amaterasu]][[eu:Amaterasu]][[fa:آماتراسو]][[fi:Amaterasu]][[fr:Amaterasu]][[gl:Amaterasu]][[he:אמטראסו]][[hr:Amaterasu]][[hu:Amateraszu Ómikami]][[hy:Ամատերասու Օմիկամի]][[id:Amaterasu]][[it:Amaterasu]][[ja:天照大神]][[jv:Amaterasu]][[ka:ამატერასუ]][[ko:아마테라스 오미카미]][[la:Amaterasu]][[lb:Amaterasu]][[lt:Amaterasu]][[lv:Amaterasu]][[min:Amaterasu]][[ml:അമതേരസു]][[mni:ꯑꯃꯇꯦꯔꯁꯨ ꯑꯣꯃꯤꯀꯃꯤ]][[ms:Amaterasu]][[nl:Amaterasu]][[nn:Amaterasu]][[no:Amaterasu]][[or:ଆମାତେରାଶୁ]][[pa:ਆਮਾਤੇਰਸ]][[pl:Amaterasu]][[pt:Amaterasu]][[ro:Amaterasu]][[ru:Аматэрасу]][[sco:Amaterasu]][[sh:Amaterasu]][[simple:Amaterasu]][[sk:Amaterasu Ómikami]][[sl:Amaterasu]][[sr:Аматерасу]][[su:Amaterasu]][[sv:Amaterasu]][[ta:அமதெரசு]][[th:อามาเตราซุ]][[tl:Amaterasu]][[tr:Amaterasu]][[uk:Аматерасу]][[uz:Amaterasu]][[vi:Amaterasu]][[wuu:天照大神]][[zh-min-nan:Amaterasu]][[zh-yue:天照大神]][[zh:天照大神]] {{Short description|Sun goddess in Shinto|qq=}} {{Other uses|Amaterasu (disambiguation)|天照 (disambiguation)|qq=}} {{Infobox deity | type = Japanese | name = Amaterasu | other_names = Amaterasu-Ōmikami (天照大御神, 天照大神)
Amaterasu Ōkami (天照大神)

Amaterashimasu Sume(ra) Ōmikami (天照坐皇大御神)
Amaterasu Ōhirume no Mikoto (天照大日孁尊)
Ōhirume no Muchi no Kami (大日孁貴神)
Ōhirume no Mikoto (大日孁尊)
Hi no Kami (日神)
Tsukisakaki Izu no Mitama Amazakaru Mukatsuhime no Mikoto (撞賢木厳之御魂天疎向津媛命)
Tenshō Kōtaijin (天照皇大神)
Tenshō Daijin (天照大神) | god_of = Goddess of the {{ill|Sun|en|Sun|qid=Q525|lt=Sun|1=Sun}} and the {{ill|Universe|en|Universe|qid=Q1|lt=Universe|1=Universe}}; the mythical ancestress of the {{ill|Imperial House of Japan|en|Imperial House of Japan|qid=Q909452|lt=Imperial House of Japan|1=Imperial House of Japan}} | image =File:Amaterasu_cave_crop.jpg; File:Amaterasu cave crop.jpg| caption = Amaterasu emerging from the cave, [[:en:Ama-no-Iwato|Ama-no-Iwato]], to which she once retreated (detail of woodblock print by {{ill|Kunisada|en|Kunisada|qid=Q467427|lt=Kunisada|1=Kunisada}}) | planet = {{ill|Sun|en|Sun|qid=Q525|lt=Sun|1=Sun}} | consort =Unamed sun god,(One Myth),Tsukuyomi (some myths).; {{ill|Susanoo|qid=Q272993|1=Susanoo-no-Mikoto}}| parents =[[Izanagi]] ({{Lang|ja-latn|[[Kojiki]]|qq=}})
[[Izanagi]] and {{ill|Izanami|en|Izanami|qid=Q682306|lt=Izanami|1=Izanami}} ({{Lang|ja-latn|[[Nihon Shoki]]|qq=}}); {{ill|Izanagi|qid=Q813858|1=Izanagi}}; {{ill|Izanami|qid=Q682306|1=Izanami}}| siblings = [[Tsukuyomi-no-Mikoto|Tsukuyomi]]
[[Susanoo-no-Mikoto|Susanoo]]
(and [[Kamiumi|others]]) | children = {{ill|Ame-no-oshihomimi|en|Ame-no-oshihomimi|qid=Q462555|lt=Ame-no-Oshihomimi|1=Ame-no-oshihomimi}}
[[Ame no Hohi]]
[[Amatsuhikone]]
{{ill|Ikutsuhikone|en|Ikutsuhikone|qid=Q11147583|lt=Ikutsuhikone|1=Ikutsuhikone}}
[[Kumanokusubi]] | texts = {{Lang|ja-latn|[[Kojiki]]|qq=}}, {{Lang|ja-latn|[[Nihon Shoki]]|qq=}}, ''[[Kujiki|Sendai Kuji Hongi]]'' | gender = {{ill|female|qid=Q6581072|1=Female (gender)}} | offspring = {{ill|Amatsuhikone|qid=Q10940228|1=Amatsuhikone}}; {{ill|Ame no Hohi|qid=Q10940685|1=Ame no Hohi}}; {{ill|Ichikishimahime|qid=Q11059454|1=Ichikishima-hime}}; {{ill|Ikutsuhikone|qid=Q11147583|1=Ikutsuhikone}}; {{ill|Kumanokusubi|qid=Q11298796|1=Kumanokusubi}}; {{ill|Oshihomimi|qid=Q462555|1=Ame-no-oshihomimi}}; {{ill|Tagitsuhime|qid=Q10932592|1=Tagitsuhime}}; {{ill|Takiribime|qid=Q10932870|1=Tagorihime}} | venerated_in = {{ill|Shinto|qid=Q812767|1=Shinto}} | member_of = {{ill|Chijin Godai|qid=Q18337308|1=Five Generations of Earth Deities}} | deity_of = Kami of {{ill|Sun|qid=Q525|1=Sun}} | equivalent5_type = Japanese | equivalent5 = {{ill|Eleven-Faced Avalokiteśvara|qid=Q11404731|1=Ekādaśamukha}}; {{ill|Q11616186|qid=Q11616186|1=Nyakuichi Ōji}} }} '''Amaterasu Ōmikami''' ({{lang|ja|天照大御神, 天照大神|qq=}}), often called '''Amaterasu''' for short, also known as '''Ōhirume no Muchi no Kami''' ({{lang|ja|大日孁貴神|qq=}}), is the {{ill|Solar deity|en|Solar deity|qid=Q511056|lt=goddess of the sun|1=Solar deity}} in [[Japanese mythology]]. Often considered the chief [[Kami|deity]] (''kami'') of the [[Shinto]] pantheon,{{cite book |last1=Varley |first1=Paul |title=Japanese Culture |date=1 March 2000 |publisher=University of Hawaii Press |isbn=978-0-8248-6308-1 |page=8 |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=lZ0BEAAAQBAJ |access-date=1 January 2024 |language=en|qq=}}{{cite book |last1=Narayanan |first1=Vasudha |title=Eastern Religions: Origins, Beliefs, Practices, Holy Texts, Sacred Places |date=2005 |publisher=Oxford University Press |isbn=978-0-19-522191-6 |page=440 |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=Snp0vXCTFqEC&pg=PA440 |access-date=1 January 2024 |language=en|qq=}}{{cite book |last1=Zhong |first1=Yijiang |title=The Origin of Modern Shinto in Japan: The Vanquished Gods of Izumo |date=6 October 2016 |publisher=Bloomsbury Publishing |isbn=978-1-4742-7110-3 |pages=2, 3 |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=eLrMDAAAQBAJ&pg=PA2 |access-date=1 January 2024 |language=en|qq=}} she is also portrayed in Japan's earliest literary texts, the {{Lang|ja-latn|[[Kojiki]]|qq=}} ({{Circa|712 CE|qq=}}) and the {{Lang|ja-latn|[[Nihon Shoki]]|qq=}} (720 CE), as the ruler (or one of the rulers) of the heavenly realm [[Takamagahara]] and the mythical ancestress of the {{ill|Imperial House of Japan|en|Imperial House of Japan|qid=Q909452|lt=Imperial House of Japan|1=Imperial House of Japan}} via her grandson [[Ninigi-no-Mikoto|Ninigi]]. Along with her siblings, the moon deity [[Tsukuyomi-no-Mikoto|Tsukuyomi]] and the impetuous storm god [[Susanoo-no-Mikoto|Susanoo]], she is considered to be one of the "Three Precious Children" ({{lang|ja|三貴子|qq=}}, {{transliteration|ja|mihashira no uzu no miko / sankishi|qq=}}), the three most important offspring of the creator god [[Izanagi]]. Amaterasu's chief place of worship, the [[Ise Grand Shrine|Grand Shrine of Ise]] in {{ill|Ise, Mie|en|Ise, Mie|qid=Q328067|lt=Ise|1=Ise, Mie}}, {{ill|Mie Prefecture|en|Mie Prefecture|qid=Q128196|lt=Mie Prefecture|1=Mie Prefecture}}, is one of Shinto's holiest sites and a major pilgrimage center and tourist spot. As with other Shinto ''kami'', she is also enshrined in a number of {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|qid=Q845945|lt=Shinto shrine|1=Shinto shrine}}s throughout Japan. ==Name== The goddess is referred to as 'Amaterasu Ōmikami' ({{lang|ja|天照大御神|qq=}} / {{lang|ja|天照大神|qq=}}; {{ill|Historical kana orthography|en|Historical kana orthography|qid=Q1142552|lt=historical orthography|1=Historical kana orthography}}: {{lang|ja|あまてらすおほみかみ|qq=}}, ''Amaterasu Ohomikami''; {{ill|Old Japanese|en|Old Japanese|qid=Q5736700|lt=Old Japanese|1=Old Japanese}}: ''Amaterasu Opomi[[Old Japanese#Syllables|1]]kami2'') in the {{Lang|ja-latn|[[Kojiki]]|qq=}}, while the {{Lang|ja-latn|[[Nihon Shoki]]|qq=}} gives the following variant names: *Ōhirume-no-Muchi ({{lang|ja|大日孁貴|qq=}}; {{ill|Man'yōgana|en|Man'yōgana|qid=Q862803|lt=Man'yōgana|1=Man'yōgana}}: {{lang|ja|於保比屢咩能武智|qq=}}; hist. orthography: {{lang|ja|おほひるめのむち|qq=}}, ''Ohohirume-no-Muchi''; Old Japanese: ''Opopi1rume1-no2-Muti''){{cite book |last1=Kuroita |first1=Katsumi |title=Kundoku Nihon Shoki, vol. 1 (訓読日本書紀 上巻) |date=1943 |publisher=Iwanami Shoten |pages=27 |url=https://dl.ndl.go.jp/info:ndljp/pid/1904260/151|qq=}}{{cite wikisource |author-first= William George |author-last= Aston |chapter= Book I |wslink= Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697 |plaintitle= Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697 |year= 1896 |publisher= Kegan Paul, Trench, Trübner & Co.|wspage=18|qq=}} *Amaterasu Ō(mi)kami ({{Lang|ja|天照大神|qq=}}; hist. orthography: {{Lang|ja|あまてらすおほ(み)かみ|qq=}}, ''Amaterasu Oho(mi)kami'') *Amaterasu Ōhirume no Mikoto ({{lang|ja|天照大日孁尊|qq=}}) *Hi-no-Kami ({{lang|ja|日神|qq=}}; OJ: ''Pi1-no-Kami2'') 'Amaterasu' is thought to derive from the verb ''amateru'' "to illuminate / shine in the sky" (''[[:wikt:天#Etymology 1|ama]]'' "sky, heaven" + ''[[:wikt:照る#Japanese|teru]]'' "to shine") combined with the honorific [[Japanese grammar#Auxiliary verbs|auxiliary verb]] ''[[:wikt:す#Etymology 2|-su]]'',{{cite encyclopedia| editor=Akira Matsumura| encyclopedia=[[Daijirin]]| language=ja| edition= 2nd| script-title=ja:大辞林| year=1995| publisher=[[Sanseido]] Books| isbn=978-4385139005|qq=}} while 'Ōmikami' means "great august deity" (''[[:wikt:大#Etymology 2|ō]]'' "great" + honorific prefix ''[[:wikt:御#Etymology 4|mi-]]''{{Efn|Rendered as "august" by {{ill|Basil Hall Chamberlain|en|Basil Hall Chamberlain|qid=Q810029|lt=Basil Hall Chamberlain|1=Basil Hall Chamberlain}} in his translation of the ''[[Kojiki]]''.|倭国|qq=}} + ''[[kami]]''). Notably, 'Amaterasu' in 'Amaterasu Ōmikami' is not technically a name the same way 'Susanoo' in '[[Susanoo-no-Mikoto|Susa no O no Mikoto]]' or '[[Ōkuninushi]]' in 'Ōkuninushi no Kami' are. ''Amaterasu'' is an [[Japanese verb conjugation#Verb bases|attributive verb form]] that modifies the noun after it, ''ōmikami''. This epithet is therefore, much more semantically transparent than most names recorded in the ''Kojiki'' and ''Nihon Shoki'', in that it means exactly what it means, without allusion, inference or etymological opacity, literally "The Great August Goddess Who Shines in Heaven". This usage is analogous to the use of [[:en:Relative clauses|Relative clauses]] in English, only different in that Japanese clauses are placed in front of the noun they modify. This is further exemplified by (1) an alternative epithet, 'Amateru Kami' (天照神,{{cite web |url= https://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%A4%A9%E7%85%A7%E7%A5%9E-427082|title= 天照神 |website= kotobank.jp|qq=}} "The Goddess Who Shines in Heaven"), which is a plain, non-honorific version of 'Amaterasu Ōmikami', (2) alternative forms of the verb ''amaterasu'' used elsewhere, for example its [[Japanese verb conjugation#Verb bases|continuative form]] ''amaterashi'' ({{lang|ja|天|qq=}}{{lang|ja|照|qq=}}{{lang|ja|之|qq=}}) in the ''[[Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku]]'',{{cite web|url=https://ja.wikisource.org/wiki/%E6%97%A5%E6%9C%AC%E4%B8%89%E4%BB%A3%E5%AF%A6%E9%8C%B2%E3%81%AE%E5%9C%B0%E9%9C%87%E5%8F%B2%E6%96%99|title=日本三代實録の地震史料|website=ja.wikisource.org|qq=}} and (3) similar uses of attributive verb forms in certain epithets, such as [[:en:Emperor Jinmu|Emperor Jinmu]]'s ''Hatsu Kunishirasu Sumeramikoto'' (始馭天下之天皇,{{cite web |url= https://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%A7%8B%E9%A6%AD%E5%A4%A9%E4%B8%8B%E4%B9%8B%E5%A4%A9%E7%9A%87%E3%83%BB%E5%BE%A1%E8%82%87%E5%9B%BD%E5%A4%A9%E7%9A%87-359483|title= 始馭天下之天皇・御肇国天皇 |website= kotobank.jp|qq=}} "His Majesty Who First Rules the Land"). There are, still, certain verb forms that are treated as proper names, such as the [[Japanese verb conjugation#Verb bases|terminal]] negative ''fukiaezu'' in '[[Ugayafukiaezu]] no Mikoto' (鸕鷀草葺不合尊, "His Augustness, Incompletely-Thatched-with-Comoran-Feathers") Her other name, 'Ōhirume', is usually understood as meaning "great woman of the sun / daytime" (cf. ''[[:wikt:昼#Etymology 2|hiru]]'' "day(time), noon", from ''[[:wikt:日#Etymology 3 2|hi]]'' "sun, day" + ''[[:wikt:女#Etymology 3|me]]'' "woman, lady"),{{cite web |last1=Tatsumi |first1=Masaaki |title=天照らす日女の命 (Amaterasuhirumenomikoto) |url=http://k-amc.kokugakuin.ac.jp/DM/detail.do?class_name=col_dsg&data_id=68210 |website=万葉神事語辞典 |publisher=Kokugakuin University |access-date=2020-10-11 |language=ja |archive-date=2020-10-11 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20201011084255/http://k-amc.kokugakuin.ac.jp/DM/detail.do?class_name=col_dsg&data_id=68210 |url-status=dead |qq=}}{{cite journal |last1=Naumann |first1=Nelly |title='Sakahagi': The 'Reverse Flaying' of the Heavenly Piebald Horse |journal=Asian Folklore Studies |year=1982 |volume=41 |issue=1 |pages=26–27 |doi=10.2307/1178306 |jstor=1178306 |qq=}}{{cite journal |last1=Akima |first1=Toshio |title=The Myth of the Goddess of the Undersea World and the tale of Empress Jingū's Subjugation of Silla |journal=Japanese Journal of Religious Studies |date=1993 |volume=20 |issue=2–3 |pages=120–121 |url=https://nirc.nanzan-u.ac.jp/nfile/2513 |publisher=Nanzan Institute for Religion and Culture|doi=10.18874/jjrs.20.2-3.1993.95-185 |doi-access=free |qq=}} though alternative etymologies such as "great spirit woman" (taking ''hi'' to mean "spirit") or "wife of the sun" (suggested by [[:en:Orikuchi Shinobu|Orikuchi Shinobu]], who put forward the theory that Amaterasu was originally conceived of as the consort or priestess of a male solar deity) had been proposed.{{cite book |editor1-last=Eliade |editor1-first=Mircea |title=The Encyclopedia of Religion, vol. 1 |date=1987 |publisher=Macmillan |page=228 |chapter=Amaterasu|qq=}}Akima (1993). p. 172.{{cite book |last1=Matsumura |first1=Kazuo |title=Mythical Thinkings: What Can We Learn from Comparative Mythology? |date=2014 |publisher=Countershock Press |page=118 |isbn=9781304772534 |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=skh8BwAAQBAJ&pg=PA118|qq=}} A possible connection with the name {{ill|Ebisu (mythology)|en|Ebisu (mythology)|qid=Q1129330|lt=Ebisu (mythology)|1=Ebisu (mythology)}} (the child rejected by the gods [[Izanagi]] and {{ill|Izanami|en|Izanami|qid=Q682306|lt=Izanami|1=Izanami}} and one of Amaterasu's siblings) has also been suggested.{{cite book |last1=Wachutka |first1=Michael |title=Historical Reality Or Metaphoric Expression?: Culturally Formed Contrasts in Karl Florenz' and Iida Takesato's Interpretations of Japanese Mythology |date=2001 |publisher=LIT Verlag Münster |pages=113–114|qq=}} To this name is appended the honorific ''muchi'',{{cite web |title=貴（むち） |url=https://dictionary.goo.ne.jp/word/%E8%B2%B4_%28%E3%82%80%E3%81%A1%29/ |website=goo国語辞書 |access-date=2020-10-11 |language=ja|qq=}} which is also seen in a few other {{ill|Theonym|en|Theonym|qid=Q12160552|lt=Theonym|1=Theonym}}s such as '[[Ōkuninushi|Ō(a)namuchi]]'{{cite book |last1=Tsugita |first1=Uruu |title=Shinpan Norito Shinkō (新版祝詞新講) |date=2008 |publisher=Ebisu Kōshō Shuppan |pages=506–507 |isbn=9784900901858 |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=b3aYegMdAPMC|qq=}} or 'Michinushi-no-Muchi' (an epithet of the [[Munakata Taisha|three Munakata goddesses]]{{cite wikisource |author-first= William George |author-last= Aston |chapter= Book I |wslink= Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697 |plaintitle= Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697 |year= 1896 |publisher= Kegan Paul, Trench, Trübner & Co.|wspage=40|qq=}}). As the ancestress of the imperial line, the epithet 'Sume(ra)-Ō(mi)kami' ({{Lang|ja|皇大神|qq=}}, lit. "great imperial deity"; [[Kanji#On'yomi (Sino-Japanese reading)|also read as]] 'Kōtaijin'{{cite web |title=皇大神 |url=https://kotobank.jp/word/%E7%9A%87%E5%A4%A7%E7%A5%9E-496430 |website=Kotobank|script-website=ja:コトバンク |publisher=The Asahi Shimbun Company |access-date=2020-10-11|qq=}}) is also applied to Amaterasu in names such as 'Amaterasu Sume(ra) Ō(mi)kami' ({{Lang|ja|天照皇大神|qq=}}, also read as 'Tenshō Kōtaijin'){{cite book |last1=Tobe |first1=Tamio |title="Nihon no kami-sama" ga yoku wakaru hon: yaoyorozu no kami no kigen / seikaku kara go-riyaku made o kanzen gaido (「日本の神様」がよくわかる本: 八百万神の起源・性格からご利益までを完全ガイド) |date=2004 |publisher=PHP Kenkyūsho |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=CK27BQAAQBAJ&pg=PR5|qq=}}{{cite book |last1=Nagasawa |first1=Rintarō |title=Kōso kōsō no seiseki (皇祖皇宗之聖蹟) |date=1917 |publisher=Shinreikaku |page=1 |url=https://dl.ndl.go.jp/info:ndljp/pid/913588/9|qq=}} and 'Amaterashimasu-Sume(ra)-Ōmikami' ({{Lang|ja|天照坐皇大御神|qq=}}).{{cite web |title=天照大御神(アマテラスオオミカミ) |url=https://www.kyototuu.jp/Jinjya/MikotoAmaterasu.html |website=京都通百科事典 (Encyclopedia of Kyoto) |access-date=2020-10-11|qq=}} During the medieval and early modern periods, the deity was also referred to as 'Tenshō Daijin' (the ''[[Kanji#On'yomi (Sino-Japanese reading)|on'yomi]]'' of {{Lang|ja|天照大神|qq=}}) or 'Amateru Ongami' (an alternate reading of the same).{{cite book |author1=Teeuwen, Mark |editor1-last=Scheid |editor1-first=Bernhard |editor2-last=Teeuwen |editor2-first=Mark |title=The Culture of Secrecy in Japanese Religion |date=2015 |publisher=Routledge |page=1999 |chapter-url=https://books.google.com/books?id=3Lk7lt70qDcC&pg=PA199 |chapter=Knowing vs. owning a secret: Secrecy in medieval Japan, as seen through the ''sokui kanjō'' enthronement unction|isbn=9781134168743 |qq=}}{{cite book |last1=Kaempfer |first1=Engelbert |title=Kaempfer's Japan: Tokugawa Culture Observed |date=1999 |publisher=University of Hawaii Press |page=52 |isbn=9780824820664 |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=H2xdLbvCR6sC&pg=PA52|translator-last=Bodart-Bailey |translator-first=Beatrice M.|qq=}}{{cite book |last1=Hardacre |first1=Helen |title=Kurozumikyo and the New Religions of Japan |date=1988 |publisher=Princeton University Press |page=53 |isbn=0691020485 |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=veE9DwAAQBAJ&pg=PA53|qq=}}{{cite book |last1=Bocking |first1=Brian |title=The Oracles of the Three Shrines: Windows on Japanese Religion |date=2013 |publisher=Routledge |isbn=9781136845451 |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=sUldAgAAQBAJ&pg=PA49|qq=}} The name 'Amaterasu Ōmikami' has been translated into English in different ways. While a number of authors such as [[Don Philippi|Donald Philippi]] rendered it as "heaven-illuminating great deity,"{{cite book |last1=Philippi |first1=Donald L. |title=Kojiki. Translated with an Introduction and Notes |date=1968 |publisher=University of Tokyo Press |page=454|qq=}} {{ill|Basil Hall Chamberlain|en|Basil Hall Chamberlain|qid=Q810029|lt=Basil Hall Chamberlain|1=Basil Hall Chamberlain}} argued (citing the authority of {{ill|Motoori Norinaga|en|Motoori Norinaga|qid=Q384647|lt=Motoori Norinaga|1=Motoori Norinaga}}) that it is more accurately understood to mean "shining in heaven" (because the auxiliary ''su'' is merely honorific, not causative, such interpretation as "to make heaven shine" would miss the mark), and accordingly translated it as "Heaven-Shining-Great-August-Deity".Chamberlain (1882). [https://www.sacred-texts.com/shi/kj/kj017.htm Section XI.—Investiture of the Three Deities; The Illustrious August Children.] Gustav Heldt's 2014 translation of the ''Kojiki'', meanwhile, renders it as "the great and mighty spirit Heaven Shining."{{cite book |last1=Heldt |first1=Gustav |title=The Kojiki: An Account of Ancient Matters |date=2014 |publisher=Columbia University Press |isbn=978-0-2311-6388-0 |pages=xiv, 18|qq=}} ==Mythology== ===In classical mythology=== ====Birth==== [[File:阿波岐原 - Awagihara.jpg|thumb|150px|[[Izanagi]] purifying himself (''[[misogi]]'') by immersing in the Tachibana River ({{ill|Natori Shunsen|en|Natori Shunsen|qid=Q2344286|lt=Natori Shunsen|1=Natori Shunsen}})]] [[File:Installation of the Sun Goddess (Amaterasu) c1870 after Kawanabe Kyosai.jpg|thumb|Installation of the Sun Goddess (Amaterasu)]] Both the {{Lang|ja-latn|[[Kojiki]]|qq=}} ({{Circa|712 CE|qq=}}) and the {{Lang|ja-latn|[[Nihon Shoki]]|qq=}} (720 CE) agree in their description of Amaterasu as the daughter of the god [[Izanagi]] and the elder sister of [[Tsukuyomi-no-Mikoto|Tsukuyomi]], the deity of the {{ill|Moon|en|Moon|qid=Q405|lt=Moon|1=Moon}}, and [[Susanoo-no-Mikoto|Susanoo]], the god of storms and seas. The circumstances surrounding the birth of these three deities, known as the "Three Precious Children" ({{Lang|ja|三貴子|qq=}}, ''mihashira no uzu no miko'' or ''sankishi''), however, vary between sources: *In the {{Lang|ja-latn|[[Kojiki]]|qq=}}, Amaterasu, Tsukuyomi and Susanoo were born when Izanagi went to "[the plain of] Awagihara by the river-mouth of Tachibana in {{ill|Hyūga Province|en|Hyūga Province|qid=Q513737|lt=Himuka|1=Hyūga Province}} in [the island of] [[Kyushu|Tsukushi]]"{{efn|'Awagihara' or 'Awakihara' ({{ill|Old Japanese|en|Old Japanese|qid=Q5736700|lt=Old Japanese|1=Old Japanese}}: ''Apaki1para'') is a toponym meaning "a plain covered with ''{{ill|en:Lespedeza bicolor|en|en:Lespedeza bicolor|qid=Q232652|lt=awagi|1=Lespedeza bicolor}}'' shrubs". Its actual location is considered uncertain,{{cite book |last1=Philippi |first1=Donald L. |title=Kojiki |date=2015 |publisher=Princeton University Press |isbn=978-1400878000 |pages=462–463|qq=}} although a pond near [[Eda Shrine]] in modern-day Awakigahara-chō, [[:en:Miyazaki, Miyazaki|Miyazaki]], {{ill|Miyazaki Prefecture|en|Miyazaki Prefecture|qid=Q130300|lt=Miyazaki Prefecture|1=Miyazaki Prefecture}} (corresponding to the historical {{ill|Hyūga Province|en|Hyūga Province|qid=Q513737|lt=Himuka / Hyūga Province|1=Hyūga Province}}) is identified in local lore as the exact spot where Izanagi purified himself.{{cite web |title=みそぎ祓（はら）いのルーツ・阿波岐原 |url=http://mppf.or.jp/awaki/myth/ |website=Awakigahara Forest Park |access-date=2022-02-12|qq=}}{{cite web |title=阿波岐原 |url=http://kojiki.kokugakuin.ac.jp/chimei/awakihara/ |website=國學院大學 古事記学センター |publisher=Kokugakuin University |language=Japanese|qq=}}|qq=}} and [[Misogi|bathed]] (''misogi'') in the river to purify himself after visiting {{ill|Yomi|en|Yomi|qid=Q1142558|lt=Yomi|1=Yomi}}, the underworld, in a failed attempt to rescue his deceased wife, {{ill|Izanami|en|Izanami|qid=Q682306|lt=Izanami|1=Izanami}}. Amaterasu was born when Izanagi washed his left eye, Tsukuyomi was born when he washed his right eye, and Susanoo was born when he washed his nose. Izanagi then appoints Amaterasu to rule [[Takamagahara]] (the "Plain of High Heaven"), Tsukuyomi the night, and Susanoo the seas.{{cite book |last1=Philippi |first1=Donald L. |title=Kojiki |date=2015 |publisher=Princeton University Press |isbn=978-1400878000 |pages=68–71|qq=}}Chamberlain (1882). [http://sacred-texts.com/shi/kj/kj018.htm Section XI.—Investiture of the Three Deities; The Illustrious August Children.]Chamberlain (1882). [http://sacred-texts.com/shi/kj/kj019.htm Section XII.—The Crying and Weeping of His Impetuous-Male-Augustness.] *The main narrative of the {{Lang|ja-latn|[[Nihon Shoki]]|qq=}} has Izanagi and Izanami procreating after [[Kuniumi|creating the Japanese archipelago]]; to them were born (in the following order) Ōhirume-no-Muchi (Amaterasu), Tsukuyomi, the 'leech-child' {{ill|Ebisu (mythology)|en|Ebisu (mythology)|qid=Q1129330|lt=Ebisu (mythology)|1=Ebisu (mythology)}}, and Susanoo: {{blockquote|After this Izanagi no Mikoto and Izanami no Mikoto consulted together, saying:—"We have now produced the Great-eight-island country, with the mountains, rivers, herbs, and trees. Why should we not produce someone who shall be lord of the universe?" They then together produced the Sun-Goddess, who was called Oho-hiru-me no muchi. [...]
The resplendent lustre of this child shone throughout all the six quarters. Therefore the two Deities rejoiced, saying:—"We have had many children, but none of them have been equal to this wondrous infant. She ought not to be kept long in this land, but we ought of our own accord to send her at once to Heaven, and entrust to her the affairs of Heaven."
At this time Heaven and Earth were still not far separated, and therefore they sent her up to Heaven by the ladder of Heaven.|qq=}} *A variant legend recorded in the ''Shoki'' has Izanagi begetting Ōhirume (Amaterasu) by holding a {{ill|Bronze mirror|en|Bronze mirror|qid=Q1041984|lt=Bronze mirror|1=Bronze mirror}} in his left hand, Tsukuyomi by holding another mirror in his right hand, and Susanoo by turning his head and looking sideways.{{cite wikisource |author-first= William George |author-last= Aston |chapter= Book I |wslink= Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697 |plaintitle= Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697 |year= 1896 |publisher= Kegan Paul, Trench, Trübner & Co.|wspage=20|qq=}} *A third variant in the ''Shoki'' has Izanagi and Izanami begetting the sun, the moon, Hiruko, and Susanoo, as in the main narrative. Their final child, the fire god [[Kagu-tsuchi|Kagutsuchi]], caused Izanami's death (as in the ''Kojiki''). *A fourth variant relates a similar story to that found in the ''Kojiki'', wherein the three gods are born when Izanagi washed himself in the river of Tachibana after going to Yomi.{{cite wikisource |author-first= William George |author-last= Aston |chapter= Book I |wslink= Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697 |plaintitle= Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697 |year= 1896 |publisher= Kegan Paul, Trench, Trübner & Co.|wspage=28|qq=}} ====Amaterasu and Tsukuyomi==== One of the variant legends in the ''Shoki'' relates that Amaterasu ordered her brother Tsukuyomi to go down to the terrestrial world ([[Ashihara no Nakatsukuni|Ashihara-no-Nakatsukuni]], the "Central Land of Reed-Plains") and visit the goddess {{ill|Ukemochi|en|Ukemochi|qid=Q2280323|lt=Ukemochi|1=Ukemochi}}. When Ukemochi {{ill|Dema Deity|en|Dema Deity|qid=Q3705251|lt=vomited foodstuffs out of her mouth|1=Dema Deity}} and presented them to Tsukuyomi at a banquet, a disgusted and offended Tsukuyomi slew her and went back to Takamagahara. This act upset Amaterasu, causing her to split away from Tsukuyomi, thus separating night from day. Amaterasu then sent another god, Ame-no-Kumahito ({{Lang|ja|天熊人|qq=}}), who found various food-crops and animals emerging from Ukemochi's corpse. {{blockquote|On the crown of her head there had been produced the {{ill|Ox|en|Ox|qid=Q473194|lt=Ox|1=Ox}} and the {{ill|Horse|en|Horse|qid=Q726|lt=Horse|1=Horse}}; on the top of her forehead there had been produced {{ill|Millet|en|Millet|qid=Q259438|lt=Millet|1=Millet}}; over her eyebrows there had been produced the [[:en:Silkworm|Silkworm]]; within her eyes there had been produced {{ill|Panicum|en|Panicum|qid=Q934037|lt=panic|1=Panicum}}; in her belly there had been produced {{ill|Rice|en|Rice|qid=Q5090|lt=Rice|1=Rice}}; in her genitals there had been produced {{ill|Wheat|en|Wheat|qid=Q15645384|lt=Wheat|1=Wheat}}, large beans and small beans.{{cite wikisource |author-first= William George |author-last= Aston |chapter= Book I |wslink= Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697 |plaintitle= Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697 |year= 1896 |publisher= Kegan Paul, Trench, Trübner & Co.|wspage=32-33|qq=}}|qq=}} Amaterasu had the grains collected and sown for humanity's use and, putting the silkworms in her mouth, reeled thread from them. From this began {{ill|Agriculture|en|Agriculture|qid=Q11451|lt=Agriculture|1=Agriculture}} and {{ill|Sericulture|en|Sericulture|qid=Q864650|lt=Sericulture|1=Sericulture}}.{{cite book| last = Roberts| first = Jeremy| title = Japanese Mythology A To Z| location = New York| publisher = [[Chelsea House Publishers]]| year = 2010| edition = 2nd| url = http://www.enryo.ro/carti/Japanese%20mythology%20A%20to%20Z.pdf| isbn = 978-1604134353| access-date = 2012-04-04| archive-url = https://web.archive.org/web/20171119221829/http://www.enryo.ro/carti/Japanese%20mythology%20A%20to%20Z.pdf| archive-date = 2017-11-19| url-status = dead|qq=}} This account is not found in the ''Kojiki'', where a similar story is instead told of Susanoo and the goddess [[:en:Ōgetsuhime|Ōgetsuhime]].Chamberlain (1882). [http://sacred-texts.com/shi/kj/kj024.htm Section XVII.—The August Expulsion of His-Impetuous-Male-Augustness.] ====Amaterasu and Susanoo==== {{see also|Susanoo-no-Mikoto#Susanoo and Amaterasu|qq=}} When Susanoo, the youngest of the three divine siblings, was expelled by his father Izanagi for his troublesome nature and incessant wailing on account of missing his deceased mother Izanami, he first went up to Takamagahara to say farewell to Amaterasu. A suspicious Amaterasu went out to meet him dressed in male clothing and clad in armor, at which Susanoo proposed a trial by pledge (''[[ukehi]]'') to prove his sincerity. In the ritual, the two gods each chewed and spat out an object carried by the other (in some variants, an item they each possessed). Five (or six) gods and three goddesses were born as a result; Amaterasu adopted the males as her sons and gave the females – later known as the [[Munakata Taisha|three Munakata goddesses]] – to Susanoo.Chamberlain (1882). [http://sacred-texts.com/shi/kj/kj020.htm Section XIII.—The August Oath.]{{cite book |last1=Philippi |first1=Donald L. |title=Kojiki |year=2015 |publisher=Princeton University Press |isbn=978-1400878000 |pages=72–78|qq=}}{{cite wikisource |author-first= William George |author-last= Aston |chapter= Book I |wslink= Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697 |plaintitle= Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697 |year= 1896 |publisher= Kegan Paul, Trench, Trübner & Co.|wspages=35-39|qq=}} [[File:須佐之男命の乱暴 - Susanoo's Rampage.jpg|thumb|270px|[[Susanoo-no-Mikoto|Susanoo]] throwing the heavenly horse into Amaterasu's loom]] Susanoo, declaring that he had won the trial as he had produced deities of the required gender,{{efn|Female in the ''Kojiki'', male in the ''Shoki''.|qq=}} then "raged with victory" and proceeded to wreak havoc by destroying his sister's rice fields and defecating in her palace. While Amaterasu tolerated Susanoo's behavior at first, his "misdeeds did not cease, but became even more flagrant" until one day, he bore a hole in the rooftop of Amaterasu's weaving hall and hurled the "heavenly piebald horse" ({{Lang|ja|天斑駒|qq=}}, ''ame no fuchikoma''), which he had flayed alive, into it. One of Amaterasu's weaving maidens was alarmed and struck her genitals against a {{ill|Shuttle (weaving)|en|Shuttle (weaving)|qid=Q519840|lt=weaving shuttle|1=Shuttle (weaving)}}, killing her. In response, a furious Amaterasu shut herself inside the [[:en:Ama-no-Iwato|Ame-no-Iwayato]] ({{Lang|ja|天岩屋戸|qq=}}, "Heavenly Rock-Cave Door", also known as Ama-no-Iwato), plunging heaven and earth into total darkness.Chamberlain (1882). [http://sacred-texts.com/shi/kj/kj022.htm Section XV.—The August Ravages of His Impetuous-Male-Augustness.]{{cite book |last1=Philippi |first1=Donald L. |title=Kojiki |year=2015 |publisher=Princeton University Press |isbn=978-1400878000 |page=79|qq=}} The main account in the ''Shoki'' has Amaterasu wounding herself with the shuttle when Susanoo threw the flayed horse in her weaving hall, while a variant account identifies the goddess who was killed during this incident as Wakahirume-no-Mikoto ({{lang|ja|稚日女尊|qq=}}, lit. "young woman of the sun / day(time)").{{cite wikisource |author-first= William George |author-last= Aston |chapter= Book I |wslink= Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697 |plaintitle= Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697 |year= 1896 |publisher= Kegan Paul, Trench, Trübner & Co.|wspage=46|qq=}} Whereas the above accounts identify Susanoo's flaying of the horse as the immediate cause for Amaterasu hiding herself, yet another variant in the ''Shoki'' instead portrays it to be Susanoo defecating in her seat: {{blockquote|In one writing it is said:—"The august Sun Goddess took an enclosed rice-field and made it her Imperial rice-field. Now Sosa no wo no Mikoto, in spring, filled up the channels and broke down the divisions, and in autumn, when the grain was formed, he forthwith stretched round them division ropes. Again when the Sun-Goddess was in her Weaving-Hall, he flayed alive a piebald colt and flung it into the Hall. In all these various matters his conduct was rude in the highest degree. Nevertheless, the Sun-Goddess, out of her friendship for him, was not indignant or resentful, but took everything calmly and with forbearance.
When the time came for the Sun-Goddess to celebrate the feast of first-fruits, Sosa no wo no Mikoto secretly voided excrement under her august seat in the New Palace. The Sun-Goddess, not knowing this, went straight there and took her seat. Accordingly the Sun-Goddess drew herself up, and was sickened. She therefore was enraged, and straightway took up her abode in the Rock-cave of Heaven, and fastened its Rock-door.{{cite wikisource |author-first= William George |author-last= Aston |chapter= Book I |wslink= Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697 |plaintitle= Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697 |year= 1896 |publisher= Kegan Paul, Trench, Trübner & Co.|wspage=47|qq=}}|qq=}} ====The Heavenly Rock Cave==== [[File:Origin of Iwato Kagura Dance Amaterasu by Toyokuni III (Kunisada) 1856.png|thumb|390x390px|Amaterasu emerges from the [[Amano-Iwato|Heavenly Rock Cave]], bringing sunlight back to the world (Triptych by {{ill|Kunisada|en|Kunisada|qid=Q467427|lt=Kunisada|1=Kunisada}})]] [[File:Origin of the Cave Door Dance (Amaterasu) by Shunsai Toshimasa 1889.jpg|thumb|390px|''Origin of Music and Dance at the Rock Door'' by Shunsai Toshimasa, 1887]] [[File:The Origin of Iwato Kagura Triptych (Amaterasu) by Utagawa Kunisada c1844.png|thumb|390px|The Origin of Iwato Kagura by Utagawa Kunisada c1844]] After Amaterasu hid herself in the cave, the gods, led by [[Omoikane|Omoikane]], the god of wisdom, conceived a plan to lure her out: {{blockquote|[The gods] gathered together the {{ill|Rooster|en|Rooster|qid=Q2216236|lt=long-crying birds|1=Rooster}} of Tokoyo and caused them to cry. (...) They uprooted by the very roots the flourishing ''{{ill|Cleyera japonica|en|Cleyera japonica|qid=Q775470|lt=ma-sakaki|1=Cleyera japonica}}'' trees of the mountain Ame-no-Kaguyama; to the upper branches they affixed long strings of myriad ''[[magatama]]'' beads; in the middlebranches they hung [[Yata no Kagami|a large-dimensioned mirror]]; in the lower branches they suspended white ''nikite'' cloth and blue ''nikite'' cloth.
These various objects were held in his hands by [[Futodama|Futotama-no-Mikoto]] as solemn offerings, and [[Ame-no-Koyane|Ame-no-Koyane-no-Mikoto]] intoned a solemn [[Norito|liturgy]].
{{ill|Ame-no-Tajikarao|en|Ame-no-Tajikarao|qid=Q849412|lt=Ame-no-Tajikarao-no-Kami|1=Ame-no-Tajikarao}} stood concealed beside the door, while [[Ame-no-Uzume|Ame-no-Uzume-no-Mikoto]] bound up her sleeves with a cord of heavenly ''hikage'' vine, tied around her head a head-band of the heavenly ''{{ill|Trachelospermum asiaticum|en|Trachelospermum asiaticum|qid=Q11238988|lt=masaki|1=Trachelospermum asiaticum}}'' vine, bound together bundles of ''{{ill|Sasa (plant)|en|Sasa (plant)|qid=Q2322484|lt=sasa|1=Sasa (plant)}}'' leaves to hold in her hands, and overturning a bucket before the heavenly rock-cave door, stamped resoundingly upon it. Then she became divinely possessed, exposed her breasts, and pushed her skirt-band down to her genitals.
Then Takamanohara shook as the eight-hundred myriad deities laughed at once.Translation from {{cite book |last1=Philippi |first1=Donald L. |title=Kojiki |date=2015 |publisher=Princeton University Press |isbn=978-1400878000 |pages=82–84|qq=}} Names and untranslated words (transcribed in {{ill|Old Japanese|en|Old Japanese|qid=Q5736700|lt=Old Japanese|1=Old Japanese}} in the original) have been changed into their modern equivalents.|author=|title=|source=|qq=}} Inside the cave, Amaterasu is surprised that the gods should show such mirth in her absence. Ame-no-Uzume answered that they were celebrating because another god greater than her had appeared. Curious, Amaterasu slid the boulder blocking the cave's entrance and peeked out, at which Ame-no-Koyane and Futodama brought out the mirror (the [[:en:Yata-no-Kagami|Yata-no-Kagami]]) and held it before her. As Amaterasu, struck by her own reflection (apparently thinking it to be the other deity Ame-no-Uzume spoke of), approached the mirror, Ame-no-Tajikarao took her hand and pulled her out of the cave, which was then immediately sealed with a {{ill|Shimenawa|en|Shimenawa|qid=Q1887421|lt=straw rope|1=Shimenawa}}, preventing her from going back inside. Thus was light restored to the world.{{cite book |last1=Philippi |first1=Donald L. |title=Kojiki |date=2015 |publisher=Princeton University Press |isbn=978-1400878000 |pages=82–85|qq=}}Chamberlain (1882). [http://sacred-texts.com/shi/kj/kj023.htm Section XVI.—The Door of the Heavenly Rock-Dwelling.]{{cite wikisource |author-first= William George |author-last= Aston |chapter= Book I |wslink= Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697 |plaintitle= Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697 |year= 1896 |publisher= Kegan Paul, Trench, Trübner & Co.|wspage=42|qq=}} As punishment for his unruly conduct, Susanoo was then driven out of Takamagahara by the other gods. Going down to earth, he arrived at the {{ill|Izumo Province|en|Izumo Province|qid=Q582846|lt=land of Izumo|1=Izumo Province}}, where he killed the monstrous serpent {{ill|Yamata no Orochi|en|Yamata no Orochi|qid=Q1054437|lt=Yamata no Orochi|1=Yamata no Orochi}} to rescue the goddess [[Kushinadahime]], whom he eventually married. From the serpent's carcass Susanoo found the sword Ame-no-Murakumo-no-Tsurugi ({{Lang|ja|天叢雲剣|qq=}}, "Sword of the Gathering Clouds of Heaven"), also known as [[Kusanagi no Tsurugi|Kusanagi-no-Tsurugi]] ({{Lang|ja|草薙剣|qq=}} "Grass-Cutting Sword"), which he presented to Amaterasu as a reconciliatory gift.Chamberlain (1882). [http://sacred-texts.com/shi/kj/kj025.htm Section XVIII.—The Eight-Forked Serpent.]{{cite wikisource |author-first= William George |author-last= Aston |chapter= Book I |wslink= Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697 |plaintitle= Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697 |year= 1896 |publisher= Kegan Paul, Trench, Trübner & Co.|wspages=45, 52|qq=}} ====The subjugation of Ashihara-no-Nakatsukuni==== {{see also|Ōkuninushi|Kuni-yuzuri|Tenson kōrin|qq=}} [[File:三種の神器 1200x1200.png|left|thumb|Artist's impression of the three {{ill|Imperial Regalia of Japan|en|Imperial Regalia of Japan|qid=Q327465|lt=Imperial Regalia of Japan|1=Imperial Regalia of Japan}}]] After a time, Amaterasu and the [[Kotoamatsukami|primordial deity]] [[Takamimusubi]] (also known as Takagi-no-Kami) declared that Ashihara-no-Nakatsukuni, which was then being ruled over by [[Ōkuninushi]] (also known as Ō(a)namuchi), the descendant (''Kojiki'') or the son (''Shoki'') of Susanoo, should be pacified and put under the jurisdiction of their progeny, claiming it to be teeming with "numerous deities which shone with a lustre like that of fireflies, and evil deities which buzzed like flies".{{cite wikisource |author-first= William George |author-last= Aston |chapter= Book II |wslink= Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697 |plaintitle= Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697 |year= 1896 |publisher= Kegan Paul, Trench, Trübner & Co.|wspage=64|qq=}} Amaterasu ordered {{ill|Ame-no-oshihomimi|en|Ame-no-oshihomimi|qid=Q462555|lt=Ame-no-Oshihomimi|1=Ame-no-oshihomimi}}, the firstborn of the five male children born during her contest with Susanoo, to go down to earth and establish his rule over it. However, after inspecting the land below, he deemed it to be in an uproar and refused to go any further.{{cite book |last1=Philippi |first1=Donald L. |title=Kojiki |date=2015 |publisher=Princeton University Press |isbn=978-1400878000 |pages=120–122|qq=}}Chamberlain (1882). [http://sacred-texts.com/shi/kj/kj037.htm Section XXX.—The August Deliberation for Pacifying the Land.] At the advice of Omoikane and the other deities, Amaterasu then dispatched another of her five sons, [[Ame no Hohi]]. Upon arriving, however, Ame no Hohi began to curry favor with Ōkuninushi and did not send back any report for three years. The heavenly deities then sent a third messenger, [[Ame no Wakahiko|Ame-no-Wakahiko]], who also ended up siding with Ōkuninushi and marrying his daughter Shitateruhime. After eight years, a female [[:en:Japanese green pheasant|pheasant]] was sent to question Ame-no-Wakahiko, who killed it with his bow and arrow. The blood-stained arrow flew straight up to Takamagahara at the feet of Amaterasu and Takamimusubi, who then threw it back to earth with a curse, killing Ame-no-Wakahiko in his sleep.{{cite book |last1=Philippi |first1=Donald L. |title=Kojiki |date=2015 |publisher=Princeton University Press |isbn=978-1400878000 |pages=123–125|qq=}}{{cite web|last1=Mori|first1=Mizue|title=Amewakahiko|url=http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=46|website=Encyclopedia of Shinto|publisher=Kokugakuin University|access-date=13 February 2017|archive-date=14 February 2020|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20200214230438/http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=46|url-status=dead|qq=}}{{cite wikisource |author-first= William George |author-last= Aston |chapter= Book I |wslink= Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697 |plaintitle= Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697 |year= 1896 |publisher= Kegan Paul, Trench, Trübner & Co.|wspages=65-66|qq=}} The preceding messengers having thus failed to complete their task, the heavenly gods finally sent the warrior deities [[Futsunushi]] and [[Takemikazuchi]]{{efn|So the ''Nihon Shoki'', the ''[[Kogo Shūi]]'',{{cite book |translator1-last=Katō |translator1-first=Genchi |translator2-last=Hoshino |translator2-first=Hikoshirō |title=Kogoshūi: Gleanings from Ancient Stories. Translated with an introduction and notes |date=1925 |publisher=Meiji Japan Society |page=16|qq=}} and the ''[[:en:Sendai Kuji Hongi|Sendai Kuji Hongi]]''. In the ''Kojiki'' (where Futsunushi is not mentioned), the envoys sent by the heavenly gods are Takemikazuchi and the bird-boat deity [[Kamiumi#Birth of the gods|Ame-no-Torifune]].Chamberlain (1882). [https://www.sacred-texts.com/shi/kj/kj039.htm Section XXXII.—Abdication of the Deity Master-of-the-Great-Land.]{{cite book |last1=Philippi |first1=Donald L. |title=Kojiki |date=2015 |publisher=Princeton University Press |isbn=978-1400878000 |page=129|qq=}} In the ''Izumo no Kuni no Miyatsuko no Kanʼyogoto'' ("Congratulatory Words of the ''[[Kuni no miyatsuko|Kuni no Miyatsuko]]'' of Izumo" - a ''[[norito]]'' recited by the governor of Izumo Province before the imperial court during his appointment), Futsunushi's companion is Ame-no-Oshihomimi's son Ame no Hinadori.{{cite book |editor1-last=De Bary |editor1-first=Wm. Theodore |editor2-last=Keene |editor2-first=Donald |editor3-last=Tanabe |editor3-first=George |editor4-last=Varley |editor4-first=Paul |title=Sources of Japanese Tradition: From Earliest Times to 1600 |date=2001 |publisher=Columbia University Press |page=38 |isbn=9780231121385 |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=tNJ_s2FEOWcC&pg=PA38|qq=}}{{cite journal |last1=Takioto |first1=Yoshiyuki |title=Izumo no Kuni no Miyatsuko no Kan'yogoto no Shinwa (Izumo Province 造神賀詞の神話) |journal=Komazawa Shigaku |date=2012 |volume=78 |pages=1–17 |url=http://repo.komazawa-u.ac.jp/opac/repository/all/32737/rsg078-01-takiotoyoshiyuki.pdf |archive-url=https://ghostarchive.org/archive/20221009/http://repo.komazawa-u.ac.jp/opac/repository/all/32737/rsg078-01-takiotoyoshiyuki.pdf |archive-date=2022-10-09 |url-status=live |publisher=[[Komazawa University]] |language=ja|qq=}}{{cite web |last1=Nishioka |first1=Kazuhiko |title=Amenooshihomimi |url=http://k-amc.kokugakuin.ac.jp/DM/dbSearchList.do?class_name=col_eos&search_condition_type=1&db_search_condition_type=4&View=2&startNo=1&focus_type=0&searchFreeword=Amenooshihomimi&searchRangeType=0 |website=Encyclopedia of Shinto |publisher=Kokugakuin University |access-date=2020-03-25 |qq=}}{{Dead link|date=December 2021 |bot=InternetArchiveBot |fix-attempted=yes |qq=}}|qq=}} to remonstrate with Ōkuninushi. At the advice of his son [[Kotoshironushi-no-Mikoto|Kotoshironushi]], Ōkuninushi agreed to abdicate and left the physical realm to govern the unseen spirit world, which was given to him in exchange. The two gods then went around Ashihara-no-Nakatsukuni, killing those who resisted them and rewarding those who rendered submission, before going back to heaven.{{cite wikisource |author-first= William George |author-last= Aston |chapter= Book I |wslink= Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697 |plaintitle= Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697 |year= 1896 |publisher= Kegan Paul, Trench, Trübner & Co.|wspages=68-70|qq=}} [[File:Ninigi.jpg|thumb|280px|[[Ninigi-no-Mikoto|Ninigi]] and his retinue at Mount Takachiho]] With the earth now pacified, Amaterasu and Takamimusubi again commanded Ame-no-Oshihomimi to descend and rule it. He, however, again demurred and suggested that his son [[Ninigi-no-Mikoto|Ninigi]] be sent instead. Amaterasu thus bequeathed to Ninigi, the sword Susanoo gave her, along with the two items used to lure her out of the Ame-no-Iwayato: the mirror Yata-no-Kagami and the jewel [[Yasakani no Magatama]]. With a number of gods serving as his retinue, Ninigi came down from heaven to {{ill|Takachiho, Miyazaki|en|Takachiho, Miyazaki|qid=Q1350939|lt=Mount Takachiho|1=Takachiho, Miyazaki}} in the {{ill|Hyūga Province|en|Hyūga Province|qid=Q513737|lt=land of Himuka|1=Hyūga Province}} and built his palace there. Ninigi became the ancestor of the {{ill|Imperial House of Japan|en|Imperial House of Japan|qid=Q909452|lt=emperors of Japan|1=Imperial House of Japan}}, while the mirror, jewel, and sword he brought with him became the {{ill|Imperial Regalia of Japan|en|Imperial Regalia of Japan|qid=Q327465|lt=three sacred treasures|1=Imperial Regalia of Japan}} of the imperial house. Five of the gods who accompanied him in his descent - Ame-no-Koyane, Futodama, Ame-no-Uzume, [[Ishikori-dome no Mikoto|Ishikoridome]] (the maker of the mirror), and [[:en:Tamanoya|Tamanoya]] (the maker of the jewel) - meanwhile became the ancestors of the clans involved in court ceremonial such as the [[Nakatomi clan|Nakatomi]] and the [[Inbe clan|Inbe]].Chamberlain (1882). [https://www.sacred-texts.com/shi/kj/kj040.htm Section XXXIII.—The August Descent from Heaven of His Augustness the August Grandchild.]{{cite book |last1=Philippi |first1=Donald L. |title=Kojiki |date=2015 |publisher=Princeton University Press |isbn=978-1400878000 |pages=137–141|qq=}}{{cite wikisource |author-first= William George |author-last= Aston |chapter= Book I |wslink= Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697 |plaintitle= Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697 |year= 1896 |publisher= Kegan Paul, Trench, Trübner & Co.|wspages=70, 76-77|qq=}} ====Emperor Jimmu and the Yatagarasu==== {{see also|Emperor Jimmu|qq=}} [[File:Tennō Jimmu.jpg|thumb|450px|left|{{ill|Yatagarasu|en|Yatagarasu|qid=Q745463|lt=Yatagarasu|1=Yatagarasu}} the sun crow guiding Emperor Jimmu and his men towards the plain of {{ill|Yamato Province|en|Yamato Province|qid=Q907495|lt=Yamato|1=Yamato Province}}]] Many years later, Ninigi's great-grandson, Kamuyamato-Iwarebiko (later known as {{ill|Emperor Jimmu|en|Emperor Jimmu|qid=Q200188|lt=Emperor Jimmu|1=Emperor Jimmu}}), decided to leave Himuka in search of a new home with his elder brother Itsuse. Migrating eastward, they encountered various gods and local tribes who either submitted to them or resisted them. After Itsuse died of wounds sustained during a battle against a chieftain named Nagasunehiko, Iwarebiko retreated and went to [[Kumano Region|Kumano]], located on the southern part of the [[Kii Peninsula]]. While there, he and his army were enchanted by a god in the shape of a giant bear and fell into a deep sleep. At that moment, a local named Takakuraji had a dream in which Amaterasu and Takamimusubi commanded the god Takemikazuchi to help Iwarebiko. Takemikazuchi then dropped his sword, [[Isonokami Jingu|Futsu-no-Mitama]], into Takakuraji's storehouse, ordering him to give it to Iwarebiko. Upon waking up and discovering the sword inside the storehouse, Takakuraji went to where Iwarebiko was and presented it to him. The magic power of the Futsu-no-Mitama immediately exterminated the evil gods of the region and roused Iwarebiko and his men from their slumber. Continuing their journey, the army soon found themselves stranded in the mountains. Takamimusubi (so the ''Kojiki'') or Amaterasu (''Shoki'') then told Iwarebiko in a dream that the giant crow {{ill|Yatagarasu|en|Yatagarasu|qid=Q745463|lt=Yatagarasu|1=Yatagarasu}} would be sent to guide them in their way. Soon enough, the bird appeared and led Iwarebiko and his men to safety. At length, Iwarebiko arrived at the {{ill|Yamato Province|en|Yamato Province|qid=Q907495|lt=land of Yamato|1=Yamato Province}} (modern {{ill|Nara Prefecture|en|Nara Prefecture|qid=Q131287|lt=Nara Prefecture|1=Nara Prefecture}}) and defeated Nagasunehiko, thereby avenging his brother Itsuse. He then established his palace-capital at {{ill|Kashihara, Nara|en|Kashihara, Nara|qid=Q496319|lt=Kashihara|1=Kashihara, Nara}} and ruled therein.{{cite book |last1=Philippi |first1=Donald L. |title=Kojiki |date=2015 |publisher=Princeton University Press |isbn=978-1400878000 |pages=163–177|qq=}}{{cite wikisource |author-first= William George |author-last= Aston |chapter= Book I |wslink= Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697 |plaintitle= Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697 |year= 1896 |publisher= Kegan Paul, Trench, Trübner & Co.|wspages=114-128|qq=}} ====Enshrinement in Ise==== {{see also|Ōmononushi|Saiō|qq=}} [[File:Hibara-jinja, torii-2.jpg|thumb|240px|{{ill|Ōmiwa Shrine|en|Ōmiwa Shrine|qid=Q705542|lt=Hibara Shrine|1=Ōmiwa Shrine}} at the foot of {{ill|Mount Miwa|en|Mount Miwa|qid=Q860627|lt=Mount Miwa|1=Mount Miwa}} in {{ill|Sakurai, Nara|en|Sakurai, Nara|qid=Q127026|lt=Sakurai|1=Sakurai, Nara}}, {{ill|Nara Prefecture|en|Nara Prefecture|qid=Q131287|lt=Nara Prefecture|1=Nara Prefecture}}. The shrine is identified as the place where the Yata-no-Kagami and the Kusanagi-no-Tsurugi were first enshrined after they were removed from the imperial palace.]] An anecdote concerning {{ill|Emperor Sujin|en|Emperor Sujin|qid=Q333530|lt=Emperor Sujin|1=Emperor Sujin}} relates that Amaterasu ([[Shintai|via the Yata-no-Kagami and the Kusanagi sword]]) and {{ill|Yamato Okunitama|en|Yamato Okunitama|qid=Q11387080|lt=Yamato-no-Okunitama|1=Yamato Okunitama}}, the {{ill|Tutelary deity|en|Tutelary deity|qid=Q3032312|lt=Tutelary deity|1=Tutelary deity}} of Yamato, were originally worshipped in the great hall of the imperial palace. When a series of plagues broke out during Sujin's reign, he "dreaded [...] the power of these Gods, and did not feel secure in their dwelling together." He thus entrusted the mirror and the sword to his daughter {{ill|Toyosukiiri-hime|lt=Toyosukiirihime|ja|豊鍬入姫命|1=Toyosukiiri-hime|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyosukiiri-hime|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyosukiiri-hime|qq=}}, who brought them to the village of Kasanuhi, and she would become the first [[Saiō]].{{cite web | url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=8608 | title=Encyclopedia of Shinto詳細 |qq=}} and delegated the worship of {{ill|Yamato Okunitama|en|Yamato Okunitama|qid=Q11387080|lt=Yamato-no-Okunitama|1=Yamato Okunitama}} to another daughter, Nunakiirihime. When the pestilence showed no sign of abating, he then performed {{ill|Futomani|en|Futomani|qid=Q5510530|lt=divination|1=Futomani}}, which revealed the plague to have been caused by [[Ōmononushi]], the god of {{ill|Mount Miwa|en|Mount Miwa|qid=Q860627|lt=Mount Miwa|1=Mount Miwa}}. When the god was offered proper worship as per his demands, the epidemic ceased.{{cite wikisource |author-first= William George |author-last= Aston |chapter= Book I |wslink= Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697 |plaintitle= Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697 |year= 1896 |publisher= Kegan Paul, Trench, Trübner & Co.|wspages=151-154|qq=}}{{cite book |translator1-last=Katō |translator1-first=Genchi |translator2-last=Hoshino |translator2-first=Hikoshirō |title=Kogoshūi: Gleanings from Ancient Stories. Translated with an introduction and notes |date=1925 |publisher=Meiji Japan Society |pages=29–30|qq=}} During the reign of Sujin's son and successor, {{ill|Emperor Suinin|en|Emperor Suinin|qid=Q314850|lt=Emperor Suinin|1=Emperor Suinin}}, custody of the sacred treasures were transferred from Toyosukiirihime to Suinin's daughter {{ill|Yamatohime-no-mikoto|en|Yamatohime-no-mikoto|qid=Q4388732|lt=Yamatohime|1=Yamatohime-no-mikoto}}, who took them first to "Sasahata in Uda" to the east of Miwa. Heading north to {{ill|Ōmi Province|en|Ōmi Province|qid=Q843882|lt=Ōmi|1=Ōmi Province}}, she then eastwards to {{ill|Mino Province|en|Mino Province|qid=Q864156|lt=Mino|1=Mino Province}} and proceeded south to {{ill|Ise Province|en|Ise Province|qid=Q864166|lt=Ise|1=Ise Province}}, where she received a revelation from Amaterasu: {{blockquote|Now Ama-terasu no Oho-kami instructed Yamato-hime no Mikoto, saying:—"The province of Ise, of the divine wind, is the land whither repair the waves from the eternal world, the successive waves. It is a secluded and pleasant land. In this land I wish to dwell." In compliance, therefore, with the instruction of the Great Goddess, a shrine was erected to her in the province of Ise. Accordingly an [[:en:Abstinence Palace|Abstinence Palace]] was built at Kaha-kami in Isuzu. This was called the palace of Iso. It was there that Ama-terasu no Oho-kami first descended from Heaven.{{cite wikisource |author-first= William George |author-last= Aston |chapter= Book I |wslink= Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697 |plaintitle= Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697 |year= 1896 |publisher= Kegan Paul, Trench, Trübner & Co.|wspages=176|qq=}}|qq=}} This account serves as the origin myth of the [[Ise Grand Shrine|Grand Shrine of Ise]], Amaterasu's chief place of worship. [[File:Yamato Takeru by Takahashi Yuichi (Geidai Museum).jpg|thumb|{{ill|Yamato Takeru|en|Yamato Takeru|qid=Q461258|lt=Yamato Takeru|1=Yamato Takeru}}]] Later, when Suinin's grandson Prince Ousu (also known as {{ill|Yamato Takeru|en|Yamato Takeru|qid=Q461258|lt=Yamato Takeru|1=Yamato Takeru}}) went to Ise to visit his aunt Yamatohime before going to conquer and pacify the {{ill|Kantō region|en|Kantō region|qid=Q132480|lt=eastern regions|1=Kantō region}} on the command of his father, {{ill|Emperor Keikō|en|Emperor Keikō|qid=Q329723|lt=Emperor Keikō|1=Emperor Keikō}}, he was given the divine sword to protect him in times of peril. It eventually came in handy when Yamato Takeru was lured onto an open grassland by a treacherous chieftain, who then set fire to the grass to entrap him. Desperate, Yamato Takeru used the sword to cut the grass around him (a variant in the ''Shoki'' has the sword miraculously mow the grass of its own accord) and lit a counter-fire to keep the fire away. This incident explains the sword's name ("Grass Cutter").{{cite book |last1=Philippi |first1=Donald L. |title=Kojiki |date=2015 |publisher=Princeton University Press |isbn=978-1400878000 |pages=238–240|qq=}}{{cite wikisource |author-first= William George |author-last= Aston |chapter= Book I |wslink= Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697 |plaintitle= Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697 |year= 1896 |publisher= Kegan Paul, Trench, Trübner & Co.|wspage=205|qq=}} On his way home from the east, Yamato Takeru – apparently blinded by {{ill|Hubris|en|Hubris|qid=Q735766|lt=Hubris|1=Hubris}} – left the Kusanagi in the care of his second wife, Miyazuhime of {{ill|Owari Province|en|Owari Province|qid=Q514506|lt=Owari|1=Owari Province}}, and went to confront the god of {{ill|Mount Ibuki|en|Mount Ibuki|qid=Q2624360|lt=Mount Ibuki|1=Mount Ibuki}} on his own. Without the sword's protection, he fell prey to the god's enchantment and became ill and died afterwards.{{cite book |last1=Philippi |first1=Donald L. |title=Kojiki |date=2015 |publisher=Princeton University Press |isbn=978-1400878000 |pages=245–249|qq=}}{{cite wikisource |author-first= William George |author-last= Aston |chapter= Book I |wslink= Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697 |plaintitle= Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697 |year= 1896 |publisher= Kegan Paul, Trench, Trübner & Co.|wspages=208-210|qq=}} Thus the Kusanagi stayed in Owari, where it was enshrined in the [[:en:Atsuta Jingu|shrine of Atsuta]].{{cite book |translator1-last=Katō |translator1-first=Genchi |translator2-last=Hoshino |translator2-first=Hikoshirō |title=Kogoshūi: Gleanings from Ancient Stories. Translated with an introduction and notes |date=1925 |publisher=Meiji Japan Society |page=33|qq=}} ====Empress Jingū and Amaterasu's ''aramitama''==== [[File:Hirota-jinja, haiden-2-2.jpg|thumb|left|[[Hirota Shrine]] in {{ill|Nishinomiya|en|Nishinomiya|qid=Q243853|lt=Nishinomiya|1=Nishinomiya}}, {{ill|Hyōgo Prefecture|en|Hyōgo Prefecture|qid=Q130290|lt=Hyōgo Prefecture|1=Hyōgo Prefecture}}, where Amaterasu's ''aramitama'' is enshrined]] At one time, when {{ill|Emperor Chūai|en|Emperor Chūai|qid=Q179971|lt=Emperor Chūai|1=Emperor Chūai}} was on a campaign against the [[Kumaso]] tribes of [[Kyushu]], his consort {{ill|Empress Jingū|en|Empress Jingū|qid=Q232803|lt=Jingū|1=Empress Jingū}} was possessed by unknown gods who told Chūai of a land rich in treasure located on the other side of the sea that is his for the taking. When Chūai doubted their words and accused them of being deceitful, the gods laid a curse upon him that he should die "without possessing this land." (The ''Kojiki'' and the ''Shoki'' diverge at this point: in the former, Chūai dies almost immediately after being cursed, while in the latter, he dies of a sudden illness a few months after.){{cite book |last1=Philippi |first1=Donald L. |title=Kojiki |date=2015 |publisher=Princeton University Press |isbn=978-1400878000 |pages=257–258|qq=}}{{cite wikisource |author-first= William George |author-last= Aston |chapter= Book I |wslink= Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697 |plaintitle= Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697 |year= 1896 |publisher= Kegan Paul, Trench, Trübner & Co.|wspages=221-223|qq=}} [[File:EmpressJinguInKorea.jpg|thumb|380px|{{ill|Empress Jingū|en|Empress Jingū|qid=Q232803|lt=Empress Jingū|1=Empress Jingū}}]] After Chūai's death, Jingū performed divination to ascertain which gods had spoken to her husband. The deities identified themselves as Tsukisakaki Izu no Mitama Amazakaru Mukatsuhime no Mikoto ({{Lang|ja|撞賢木厳之御魂天疎向津媛命|qq=}}, "The Awe-inspiring Spirit of the Planted ''{{ill|Cleyera japonica|en|Cleyera japonica|qid=Q775470|lt=Sakaki|1=Cleyera japonica}}'', the Lady of Sky-distant Mukatsu", usually interpreted as the ''{{ill|Mitama|en|Mitama|qid=Q495568|lt=aramitama|1=Mitama}}'' or 'violent spirit' of Amaterasu), Kotoshironushi, and the three gods of Sumie ([[:en:Sumiyoshi taisha|Sumiyoshi]]): [[Sumiyoshi sanjin|Uwatsutsunoo, Nakatsutsunoo, and Sokotsutsunoo]].{{efn|The ''Kojiki'''s account meanwhile identifies the gods as Amaterasu and the three Sumiyoshi deities.{{cite book |last1=Philippi |first1=Donald L. |title=Kojiki |date=2015 |publisher=Princeton University Press |isbn=978-1400878000 |pages=259–263|qq=}}|qq=}} Worshiping the gods in accordance with their instructions, Jingū then set out to conquer the promised land beyond the sea: the [[:en:Three kingdoms of Korea|Three kingdoms of Korea]].{{cite wikisource |author-first= William George |author-last= Aston |chapter= Book I |wslink= Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697 |plaintitle= Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697 |year= 1896 |publisher= Kegan Paul, Trench, Trübner & Co.|wspages=224-227|qq=}} When Jingū returned victorious to Japan, she enshrined the deities in places of their own choosing; Amaterasu, warning Jingū not to take her ''aramitama'' along to the capital, instructed her to install it in [[Hirota Shrine|Hirota]], the harbor where the empress disembarked.{{cite wikisource |author-first= William George |author-last= Aston |chapter= Book I |wslink= Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697 |plaintitle= Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697 |year= 1896 |publisher= Kegan Paul, Trench, Trübner & Co.|wspages=237-238|qq=}} == Family == {{Further|Family tree of Japanese deities|qq=}} === Family tree === {{chart top|Amaterasu's family tree (based on the ''Kojiki'')|qq=}} {{Tree chart/start|align=center|qq=}} {{Tree chart|A1|16=~|A2=[[Izanagi]]{{Cite (mother of Jimmu)|Tamayorihime]]{{Cite web | url=http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=151 | title=Shinto Portal - IJCC, Kokugakuin University | website=Eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp | access-date=2020-11-21 | archive-date=2011-05-19 | archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20110519004449/http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=151 | url-status=dead |qq=}}|qq=}} {{Tree chart||16=|28=|27=J|26=~|25=~|24=~|23=y|22=~|21=~|20=~|19=~|18=G1|17=|15=||14=|13=|12=|11=|10=|9=|8=|7=|6=|5=|4=||G1=[[Ugayafukiaezu]]{{cite web | archive-date=2011-05-18 | archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20110518145338/http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=161 | url-status=dead |qq=}}|qq=}} {{Tree
