chart||17=|29=|28=|27=|26=|25=|24=H1|23=|22=|21=|20=|19=|18=|16=||15=|14=|13=|12=|11=|10=|9=|8=|7=|6=|5=|4=||H1={{ill|Emperor Jimmu|en|Emperor Jimmu|qid=Q200188|lt=Emperor Jimmu|1=Emperor Jimmu}}|qq=}} {{Tree chart/end|qq=}} {{chart bottom|qq=}} === Consorts === She is a virgin goddess and never engages in sexual relationships.{{Cite web |title="Alone among Women": A Comparative Mythic Analysis of the Development of Amaterasu Theology |url=https://www2.kokugakuin.ac.jp/ijcc/wp/cpjr/kami/matsumura.html |url-status=dead |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20210126014124/http://www2.kokugakuin.ac.jp/ijcc/wp/cpjr/kami/matsumura.html |archive-date=2021-01-26 |access-date=2020-11-21 |website=2.kokugakuin.ac.jp|qq=}} However, according to {{ill|Nozomu Kawamura|ja|河村望|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nozomu Kawamura|1=Nozomu Kawamura|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11553930}}, she was a consort to a sun god{{Cite book |last=Kawamura |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=IihmAgAAQBAJ&q=virgin+amaterasu&pg=PA205 |title=Sociology & Society Of Japan |date=2013-12-19 |publisher=Routledge |isbn=978-1-317-79319-9 |pages=205 |language=en|qq=}} and some telling stories place [[Tsukuyomi-no-Mikoto|Tsukuyomi]] as her husband.{{Cite web|title=Amaterasu|url=https://mythopedia.com/japanese-mythology/gods/amaterasu/|access-date=2020-11-21|website=Mythopedia.com|language=en|qq=}} === Siblings === Amaterasu has many siblings most notably [[Susanoo-no-Mikoto|'''Susanoo''']] and [[Tsukuyomi-no-Mikoto|'''Tsukiyomi''']].{{Cite web|title=Izanagi and Izanami {{!|qq=}} Shintō deity|url=https://www.britannica.com/topic/Izanagi|website=britannica.com|access-date=2020-11-20|language=en|qq=}} {{ill|Basil Hall Chamberlain|en|Basil Hall Chamberlain|qid=Q810029|lt=Basil Hall Chamberlain|1=Basil Hall Chamberlain}} used the words "elder brother" to translate her dialog referring to Susanoo in the ''Kojiki'', even though he noted that she was his elder sister.{{Cite web | url=https://www.sacred-texts.com/shi/kj/kj020.htm | title=The Kojiki: Volume I: Section XIII.—The August Oath|website=Sacred-texts.com |qq=}} The word (which was also used by Izanami to address her elder brother and husband Izanagi) was ''nase'' (phonetically spelt {{Lang|ja|那勢|qq=}}{{Cite web|url=https://zh.wikisource.org/wiki/%E5%8F%A4%E4%BA%8B%E8%A8%98/%E4%B8%8A%E5%8D%B7|title=古事記/上卷 - 维基文库，自由的图书馆|website=Zh.wikisource.org|access-date=26 June 2022|qq=}} in the ''Kojiki''; modern dictionaries use the semantic spelling 汝兄, whose {{ill|Kanji|en|Kanji|qid=Q82772|lt=Kanji|1=Kanji}} literally mean "my elder brother"), an ancient term used only by females to refer to their brothers, who had higher status than them. (As opposed to males using {{nihongo|''nanimo''|汝妹|extra="my younger sister"|qq=}} ({{Lang|ja|那邇妹|qq=}} in the ''Kojiki'') to refer to their sisters, who had lower status than them.){{cite book|title=スーパー大辞林|trans-title=Super [[Daijirin]]|qq=}} The ''Nihon Shoki'' used the Chinese word {{Lang|zh|弟|qq=}} ("younger brother") instead.{{Cite web|url=https://zh.wikisource.org/wiki/%E6%97%A5%E6%9C%AC%E6%9B%B8%E7%B4%80/%E5%8D%B7%E7%AC%AC%E4%B8%80|title=日本書紀/卷第一 - 维基文库，自由的图书馆|website=Zh.wikisource.org|access-date=26 June 2022|qq=}} Some tellings say she had a sister named '''[[Wakahiru-me|Wakahirume]]''' who was a weaving maiden and helped Amaterasu weave clothes for the other kami in [[Takamagahara|heaven]]. Wakahirume was later accidentally killed by Susanoo.{{Cite web|title=Amaterasu|url=https://www.worldhistory.org/Amaterasu/|access-date=2020-11-20|website=[[World History Encyclopedia]]|qq=}} Other traditions say she had an older brother named '''{{ill|Ebisu (mythology)|en|Ebisu (mythology)|qid=Q1129330|lt=Hiruko|1=Ebisu (mythology)}}'''.{{Cite book|last1=Coulter|first1=Charles Russell|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=VWxekbhM1yEC&q=amaterasu&pg=PT91|title=Encyclopedia of Ancient Deities|last2=Turner|first2=Patricia|date=2013-07-04|publisher=Routledge|isbn=978-1-135-96397-2|language=en|qq=}}{{Page needed|date=September 2021|qq=}} === Descendants === {{Original research|date=September 2021|qq=}} Amaterasu has 5 sons {{ill|Ame-no-oshihomimi|en|Ame-no-oshihomimi|qid=Q462555|lt=Ame-no-oshihomimi|1=Ame-no-oshihomimi}}, [[Ame no Hohi]], [[Amatsuhikone]], {{ill|Ikutsuhikone|en|Ikutsuhikone|qid=Q11147583|lt=Ikutsuhikone|1=Ikutsuhikone}}, and [[Kumanokusubi]], who were given birth to by Susanoo by chewing her hair jewels. According to one account in the ''Nihon Shoki'', it was because these children were male that Susanoo won during the ritual to prove his intent, even though they were not his children, but hers. This explanation of the outcome of the ritual contradicts that in the ''Kojiki'', according to which it was because she gave birth to female children using his sword, and those children were his. The ''Kojiki'' claims he won because he had daughters to whom she gave birth, while the ''Nihon Shoki'' claims he won because he himself gave birth to her sons. Several figures and noble clans claim descent from Amaterasu most notably {{ill|Imperial House of Japan|en|Imperial House of Japan|qid=Q909452|lt=the Japanese imperial family|1=Imperial House of Japan}} through Emperor Jimmu who descended from her grandson [[Ninigi-no-Mikoto|Ninigi]].{{Cite book|last=Kitagawa|first=Joseph Mitsuo|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=h1xcc4cGL5cC&pg=PA145|title=On Understanding Japanese Religion|date=1987-10-21|publisher=Princeton University Press|isbn=978-0-691-10229-0|language=en|qq=}} Her son '''[[Ame no Hohi]]''' is considered the ancestral [[kami]] of clans in {{ill|Izumo Province|en|Izumo Province|qid=Q582846|lt=Izumo|1=Izumo Province}} which includes the [[Haji clan]], [[Sugawara clan]], and the [[Senge clan]]. The legendary {{ill|Sumo|en|Sumo|qid=Q40561|lt=Sumo|1=Sumo}} wrestler [[Nomi no Sukune]] is believed to be a 14th generation descendant of Amenohohi.{{Cite web|title=Encyclopedia of Shinto - Home : Kami in Classic Texts : Amenohohi|url=http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=33|access-date=2020-11-21|website=eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp|archive-date=2020-11-30|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20201130114722/http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=33|url-status=dead|qq=}}{{Cite journal|last=Borgen|first=Robert|date=1975|title=The Origins of the Sugawara. A History of the Haji Family|url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/2383977|journal=Monumenta Nipponica|volume=30|issue=4|pages=405–422|doi=10.2307/2383977|jstor=2383977|issn=0027-0741|qq=}}{{Cite book|last1=Cali|first1=Joseph|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=dNV7LoexIgoC&q=Amenohohi|title=Shinto Shrines: A Guide to the Sacred Sites of Japan's Ancient Religion|last2=Dougill|first2=John|date=2012|publisher=University of Hawaiʻi Press|isbn=978-0-8248-3713-6|language=en|qq=}}{{Cite web|title=Sumo|url=https://www.worldhistory.org/Sumo/|access-date=2020-11-21|website=[[World History Encyclopedia]]|qq=}} ==Worship== [[File:Amanoiwato Nishihongu.jpg|thumb|220px|[[Amanoiwato Shrine]] ({{lang|ja|天岩戸神社|qq=}})]] The [[Ise Grand Shrine]] ({{lang|ja|伊勢神宮|qq=}} ''Ise Jingū'') located in {{ill|Ise, Mie|en|Ise, Mie|qid=Q328067|lt=Ise|1=Ise, Mie}}, {{ill|Mie Prefecture|en|Mie Prefecture|qid=Q128196|lt=Mie Prefecture|1=Mie Prefecture}}, {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|qid=Q17|lt=Japan|1=Japan}}, houses the inner shrine, Naiku, dedicated to Amaterasu. Her sacred mirror, [[Yata no Kagami]], is said to be kept at this shrine as one of the {{ill|Imperial Regalia of Japan|en|Imperial Regalia of Japan|qid=Q327465|lt=Imperial regalia objects|1=Imperial Regalia of Japan}}.{{Cite journal|last=Ellwood|first=Robert S.|date=1968|title=Harvest and Renewal at the Grand Shrine of Ise|journal=Numen|volume=15|issue=3|pages=165–190|doi=10.2307/3269575|issn=0029-5973|jstor=3269575|qq=}} A ceremony known as {{ill|Jingū Shikinen Sengū|ja|神宮式年遷宮|1=Jingū Shikinen Sengū|1=Jingū Shikinen Sengū|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jingū Shikinen Sengū|qq=}} ({{Lang|ja|神宮式年遷宮|1=Jingū Shikinen Sengū|1=Jingū Shikinen Sengū|qq=}}) is held every twenty years at this shrine to honor the many deities enshrined, which is formed by 125 shrines altogether. New shrine buildings are built at a location adjacent to the site first. After the transfer of the object of worship, new clothing and treasure and offering food to the goddess the old buildings are taken apart. The building materials taken apart are given to many other shrines and buildings to renovate. This practice is a part of the Shinto faith and has been practiced since the year 690 CE, but is not only for Amaterasu but also for many other deities enshrined in Ise Grand Shrine.{{Cite book|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=MUBtPYKeQvEC&q=Shikinen+Sengu+690&pg=PA215|title=Demographic Change and Local Development Shrinkage, Regeneration and Social Dynamics: Shrinkage, Regeneration and Social Dynamics|last1=Cristina|first1=Martinez-Fernandez|last2=Naoko|first2=Kubo|last3=Antonella|first3=Noya|last4=Tamara|first4=Weyman|date=2012-11-28|publisher=OECD Publishing|isbn=9789264180468|language=en|qq=}} Additionally, from the late 7th century to the 14th century, an unmarried princess of the Imperial Family, called "[[Saiō]]" ({{lang|ja|斎王|qq=}}) or ''itsuki no miko'' ({{lang|ja|斎皇女|qq=}}), served as the sacred priestess of Amaterasu at the Ise Shrine upon every new dynasty.{{Cite journal|last=Takeshi|first=Matsumae|date=1978|title=Origin and Growth of the Worship of Amaterasu|journal=Asian Folklore Studies|volume=37|issue=1|pages=1–11|doi=10.2307/1177580|jstor=1177580|qq=}} The {{Nihongo|[[Amanoiwato Shrine]]|天岩戸神社|qq=}} in {{ill|Takachiho, Miyazaki|en|Takachiho, Miyazaki|qid=Q1350939|lt=Takachiho|1=Takachiho, Miyazaki}}, {{ill|Miyazaki Prefecture|en|Miyazaki Prefecture|qid=Q130300|lt=Miyazaki Prefecture|1=Miyazaki Prefecture}}, {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|qid=Q17|lt=Japan|1=Japan}} is also dedicated to Amaterasu and sits above the gorge containing [[Amano-Iwato|Ama-no-Iwato]]. The worship of Amaterasu to the exclusion of other ''kami'' has been described as "the cult of the sun."{{cite book| last = Wheeler| first = Post| title = The Sacred Scriptures of the Japanese| location = New York| publisher = Henry Schuman| pages = 393–395| year = 1952| isbn = 978-1425487874|qq=}} This phrase may also refer to the early pre-archipelagoan worship of the sun. According to the ''[[Engishiki]]'' ({{lang|ja|延喜式|qq=}}) and ''[[:en:Sandai Jitsuroku|Sandai Jitsuroku]]'' ({{lang|ja|三代実録|qq=}}) of the {{ill|Heian period|en|Heian period|qid=Q193292|lt=Heian period|1=Heian period}}, the sun goddess had many shrines named "Amateru" or "Amateru-mitama", which were mostly located in the {{ill|Kansai region|en|Kansai region|qid=Q164256|lt=Kinki|1=Kansai region}} area. However, there have also been records of a shrine on [[Tsushima Island]], coined as either "Teruhi Gongen" or the "Shining Sun Deity" during medieval times. It was later found that such a shrine was meant for a male sun deity named Ameno-himitama. Amaterasu was also once worshiped at Hinokuma shrines. The Hinokuma shrines were used to worship the goddess by the Ama people in the {{ill|Kii Province|en|Kii Province|qid=Q864053|lt=Kii Province|1=Kii Province}}s. Because the Ama people were believed to have been fishermen, researchers have conjectured that the goddess was also worshiped for a possible connection to the sea. Amaterasu was thought by some in the early 20th century until after {{ill|Pacific War|en|Pacific War|qid=Q184425|lt=Pacific War|1=Pacific War}} to have "created the [[Japanese archipelago]] from the drops of water that fell from her spear"{{Cite web |last=Chira |first=Susan |author-link=Susan Chira |date=January 7, 1989 |title=Hirohito, 124th Emperor of Japan, Is Dead at 87 |url=https://www.nytimes.com/1989/01/07/obituaries/hirohito-124th-emperor-of-japan-is-dead-at-87.html |work=[[The New York Times]]|qq=}} and in historic times, the spear was an item compared to the sun and [[:en:Solar deities|Solar deities]].{{Cite journal |last=Takeshi |first=Matsumae |year=1978 |title=Origin and Growth of the Worship of Amaterasu |url=https://asianethnology.org/downloads/ae/pdf/a318.pdf |journal=[[Asian Ethnology]] |volume=37 |issue=1 |pages=3|qq=}} == Differences in worship == Amaterasu, while primarily being the goddess of the sun, is also sometimes worshiped as having connections with other aspects and forms of nature. Amaterasu can also be considered a goddess of the wind and typhoons alongside her brother, and even possibly death.{{Cite web|url=http://web.a.ebscohost.com/ehost/pdfviewer/pdfviewer?vid=19&sid=dc46fdef-8850-424b-bf80-2fc68710bb7a%40sdc-v-sessmgr03|title=The Deity and Wind of Ise|last=Metevelis|first=Peter|qq=}}{{Dead link|date=December 2019 |bot=InternetArchiveBot |fix-attempted=yes |qq=}} There are many connections between local legends in the Ise region with other goddesses of nature, such as a nameless goddess of the underworld and sea. It's possible that Amaterasu's name became associated with these legends in the Shinto religion as it grew throughout Japan.{{Cite journal|title=The Origins of the Grand Shrine of Ise and the Cult of the Sun Goddess Amaterasu|journal=Nichibunken Japan Review|volume = 4|last=Akira|first=Toshio|doi = 10.15055/00000383|year = 1993|qq=}} One source interprets from the Heavenly Rock Cave myth that Amaterasu was seen as being responsible for the normal cycle of day and night.{{Cite book |last1=Bellingham |first1=David |url=https://www.worldcat.org/oclc/27192394 |title=Myths and Legends |last2=Whittaker |first2=Clio |last3=Grant |first3=John |publisher=Wellfleet Press |year=1992 |isbn=1-55521-812-1 |location=Secaucus, New Jersey |pages=198 |oclc=27192394|qq=}} A historical myth holds that she painted the islands of Japan into being, alongside her siblings [[Susanoo-no-Mikoto|Susanoo]] and [[Tsukuyomi-no-Mikoto|Tsukuyomi]].{{Citation needed|date=January 2024|reason=According to Encyclopedia.pub, Nature and Identity in Cross-Cultural Perspective has this story.|qq=}} In contrast, Amaterasu, while enshrined at other locations, also can be seen as the goddess that represents Japan and its ethnicity. The many differences in Shinto religion and mythology can be due to how different local gods and beliefs clashed. In the Meiji Era, the belief in Amaterasu fought against the Izumo belief in [[Ōkuninushi]] for spiritual control over the land of Japan. During this time, the religious nature of Okininushi may have been changed to be included in Shinto mythology.{{Cite web|url=http://web.a.ebscohost.com/ehost/detail/detail?vid=9&sid=dc46fdef-8850-424b-bf80-2fc68710bb7a%40sdc-v-sessmgr03&bdata=JnNpdGU9ZWhvc3QtbGl2ZQ%3d%3d#AN=BAS847138&db=bas|title=Freedom, Religion, and the Making of the Modern State in Japan, 1868-89|last=Zhong|first=Yijiang|qq=}}{{Dead link|date=December 2019 |bot=InternetArchiveBot |fix-attempted=yes |qq=}} Osagawara Shouzo built shrines in other countries to mainly spread Japan's culture and Shinto religion. It, however, was usually seen as the worshiping of Japan itself, rather than Amaterasu.{{Cite web|url=http://search.ebscohost.com/login.aspx?direct=true&db=bas&AN=BAS490582&site=ehost-live|title=Shinto Deities that Crossed the Sea: Japan's "Oversea Shrines" 1868-1945|last=Nakajima|first=Michio|qq=}} Most of these colonial and oversea shrines were destroyed after WWII.{{Cite web|url=http://web.a.ebscohost.com/ehost/pdfviewer/pdfviewer?vid=14&sid=dc46fdef-8850-424b-bf80-2fc68710bb7a%40sdc-v-sessmgr03|title=A Concept of Overseas Shinto Shrines: A Pantheistic Attempt by Ogasawara and Its Limitation|last=Suga|first=Koji|qq=}}{{Dead link|date=December 2019 |bot=InternetArchiveBot |fix-attempted=yes |qq=}} === Other worshiped forms === ==== Snake ==== Outside of being worshiped as a sun goddess, some{{who|date=September 2019|qq=}} have argued that Amaterasu was once related to snakes. There was a legend circulating among the Ise Priests that essentially described an encounter of Amaterasu sleeping with the [[Saiō]] every night in the form of a snake or lizard, evidenced by fallen scales in the priestess' bed. This was recorded by a medieval monk in his diary, which stated that "in ancient times Amaterasu was regarded as a snake deity or as a sun deity."{{Cite book|last=Kidder|first=Jonathan Edward|title=Himiko and Japan's Elusive Chiefdom of Yamatai: Archaeology, History, and Mythology|date=2007|publisher=University of Hawai'i Press|isbn=978-0-8248-3035-9|location=Honolulu|pages=265|qq=}} In the Ise kanjō, the god's snake form is considered an embodiment of the "three poisons", namely greed, anger, and ignorance.{{Cite book|last1=Breen|first1=John|title=Shinto in History: Ways of the Kami|last2=Teeuwen|first2=Mark|date=2013|publisher=Routledge|isbn=978-1-136-82704-4|location=Oxon|pages=109|qq=}} Amaterasu is also linked to a snake cult, which is also tied to the theory that the initial gender of the goddess was male. ==== Dragon ==== [[File:Mahavairocana.jpg|thumb|Amaterasu was identified with {{ill|Vairocana|en|Vairocana|qid=Q239847|lt=Vairocana|1=Vairocana}}.]] In general, some of these Amaterasu–dragon associations have been in reference to Japanese plays. One example has been within the {{ill|Chikubushima|en|Chikubushima|qid=Q2963554|lt=Chikubushima|1=Chikubushima}} tradition in which the dragon goddess [[Benzaiten]] was the emanation of Amaterasu.{{Cite book|title=Protectors and Predators: Gods of Medieval Japan, Volume 2|last=Faure|first=Bernard|s2cid=132415496|date=2015-12-31|publisher=University of Hawai'i Press|isbn=9780824839314|doi=10.21313/hawaii/9780824839314.001.0001|qq=}} Following that, in the Japanese epic, [[Taiheiki|Taiheki]], one of the characters, Nitta Yoshisada ({{lang|ja|新田義貞|qq=}}{{lrm|qq=}}), made comparisons with Amaterasu and a dragon with the quote: “I have heard that the Sun Goddess of Ise … conceals her true being in the august image of {{ill|Vairocana|en|Vairocana|qid=Q239847|lt=Vairocana|1=Vairocana}}, and that she has appeared in this world in the guise of a dragon god of the blue ocean.” Another tradition of the Heavenly Cave story depicts Amaterasu as a "dragon-fox" (''shinko'' or ''tatsugitsune'') during her descent to the famed cave because it is a type of animal/[[kami]] that emits light from its entire body.{{Cite book|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=MADlfH002mAC&q=Amaterasu+and+dragon+myth&pg=PA95|title=Shinto in History: Ways of the Kami|last1=Breen|first1=John|last2=Teeuwen|first2=Mark|date=2000|publisher=University of Hawaii Press|isbn=9780824823634|language=en|qq=}} == Relation to women's positions in early Japanese society == {{One source section | date = May 2024 }} Because Amaterasu has the highest position among the Shinto deities, there has been debate on her influence and relation to women's positions in early Japanese society. Some scholars{{who|date=September 2019|qq=}} have argued that the goddess' presence and high stature within the ''kami'' system could suggest that early rulers in Japan were female.{{Cite book|title=Japanese mythology A to Z|last=Roberts|first=Jeremy|date=2010|publisher=Chelsea House Publishers|isbn=9781438128023|edition= 2nd|location=New York, NY|oclc=540954273|qq=}} Others have argued the goddess' presence implies strong influences female priests had in Japanese politics and religion. ==See also== *{{ill|First sunrise|en|First sunrise|qid=Q11396515|lt=First sunrise|1=First sunrise}} *[[Himiko]] *[[List of solar deities]] *{{ill|Zalmoxis|en|Zalmoxis|qid=Q145218|lt=Zalmoxis|1=Zalmoxis}} *{{ill|Amaterasu (Ōkami)|en|Amaterasu (Ōkami)|qid=Q16154143|lt=Ōkami Amaterasu|1=Amaterasu (Ōkami)}} *{{ill|Tokapcup-kamuy|en|Tokapcup-kamuy|qid=Q11321134|lt=Tokapcup-kamuy|1=Tokapcup-kamuy}} *[[Shinto in popular culture]] *{{ill|Solar myths|en|Solar
